title: Gesture-Related Two-Factor Authentication for Wearable Devices via PPG Sensors

method.input_representation: Customized gesture-specific features (e.g., systolic amplitude, crest width, trough width) and general time/frequency/time-frequency features.
method.training_strategy: Enrolment phase with labeled positive samples and prestored negative samples; adaptive update scheme for long-term authentication.

evaluation.context: Calculated from 96.67% pass rate for legitimate users with security level (3, 2)

evaluation_protocol: Leave-one-subject-out style where one participant acts as the legal user and 14 others act as attackers for each gesture.

reported_limitations: Requires active user participation (stopping work to perform gesture)
Enrolment duration can be up to 80s
Not suitable for users with hand diseases (e.g., Parkinson's)

usability.usability_issues: User interaction is required, which may be intrusive in some scenarios.

key_contribution: Proposed G-PPG, a two-factor authentication system using PPG sensors to capture both heartbeat and gesture patterns.
Designed a specific feature set to capture wearer habits and physiological traits from gesture-related PPG signals.
main_strength: Robustness to movement compared to accelerometer-based authentication
Effective long-term stability through adaptive updates
main_weakness: Dependency on active user gesture performance
Lack of experimental verification against active spoofing/replay attacks
insights.notes_for_review: The 'two-factor' claim refers to the combination of physiological (heartbeat) and behavioral (gesture/habit) factors, though both are derived from a single PPG sensor.
